question: Which statements about Continuous Integration (CI) and Continuous Delivery (CD) are correct?

options.label: A
options.text: CI offers rapid feedback on code consistency and quality, with unit tests playing a crucial role.

options.label: B
options.text: CD utilizes deployment pipelines to automate testing of deployed artifacts up to production.

options.label: C
options.text: Integration tests are essential in the CD process.

options.label: D
options.text: CI eliminates the need for any testing in CD.

explanation: Per slide 4, all three are stated; CI does not eliminate the need for testing in CD.

question: What is the primary purpose of SonarQube according to the supporting tools slide?

options.label: A
options.text: It compiles Java source code into bytecode.

options.label: B
options.text: It identifies redundancy/code duplication and generates coverage reports.

options.label: C
options.text: It replaces JUnit as the unit testing framework.

options.label: D
options.text: It runs Spring Boot integration tests in Docker.

explanation: Slide 6 lists SonarQube's role in identifying redundancy, reducing complexity, and generating coverage reports.

question: Which characteristics are mentioned as unique to enterprise software systems requiring extended testing?

options.label: B
options.text: Based on the interaction of components, leading to emergent behaviors.

options.label: E
options.text: Often associated with mission-critical business functions.

explanation: Slide 8 lists all of these except the desktop-only claim.

question: Which testing technique evaluates the upper limits of a system's capacity by using a load beyond the expected maximum?
explanation: Slide 10 defines stress testing as evaluating capacity beyond expected maximum.

question: Which testing technique examines an application's entire workflow from start to finish in real user scenarios?
explanation: Slide 10 defines end-to-end testing this way.

question: Which categories of test cases should be covered according to the lecture?
explanation: Slide 12 lists positive, negative, equivalence classes, boundary values and combinations as the test case coverage categories.

question: Which naming convention follows the pattern 'MethodName_StateUnderTest_ExpectedBehavior'?
explanation: Slide 13 shows isAdult_AgeLessThan18_False as the example for MethodName_StateUnderTest_ExpectedBehavior.

question: In Mockito, what is the difference between @Mock and @InjectMocks?

options.label: A
options.text: @Mock creates a mock; @InjectMocks creates an instance of the class and injects the mocks created with @Mock.

options.label: B
options.text: Both annotations are identical in behavior.

options.label: D
options.text: @Mock is for Spring beans only and @InjectMocks for POJOs only.

explanation: Slide 14 explicitly defines this distinction.

question: Which annotations are listed as typical Spring Boot support for integration testing?
explanation: Slide 15 lists @SpringBootTest, @AutoConfigureMockMvc, and @DataJpaTest as typical Spring Boot integration test support.

question: In which Maven phase order do integration tests run?

options.label: A
options.text: validate -> compile -> test-compile -> test -> package -> integration-test -> install -> deploy

options.label: B
options.text: validate -> integration-test -> test -> package -> install -> deploy

explanation: Slide 17 specifies the Maven build lifecycle as in option A.

question: Which are phases of the Maven Failsafe plugin for integration testing?
explanation: Slide 17 lists exactly these four phases for the Failsafe plugin.

question: By default, what suffix must a Maven Failsafe integration test class name end with?
explanation: Slide 17 states test class must end with 'IT' (but can be overridden).

question: Why is mocking crucial in enterprise systems?

options.label: B
options.text: Creating a controlled environment for tests.

options.label: C
options.text: Speeding up tests via immediate responses.

options.label: E
options.text: Avoiding the need to write any assertions.

explanation: Slide 19 lists the first four reasons; mocking does not eliminate assertions.

question: According to Gerard Meszaros, which is best described as 'items passed around but never utilized, often used to fill parameter lists'?
explanation: Slide 20 defines dummy objects this way.

question: What is a 'fake' test double?

options.label: A
options.text: Working implementations that aren't suitable for production, such as an in-memory database.

options.label: B
options.text: Objects pre-programmed with expectations forming a specification of calls received.

options.label: C
options.text: Stubs that also record information on how they were called.

options.label: D
options.text: Items passed around but never used.

explanation: Slide 20: fakes have working implementations not suitable for production (e.g., in-memory DB).

question: When should you use @MockBean from Spring Boot rather than plain Mockito?

options.label: A
options.text: When the test does not need any Spring context, to keep it fast and isolated.

options.label: B
options.text: When the test needs to interact with the Spring Boot container and also mock one of its beans.

options.label: C
options.text: When you want to mock a private static method.

options.label: D
options.text: Only for unit tests of POJOs.

explanation: Slide 23: @MockBean is used when interacting with the Spring Boot container and adding/mocking a bean.

question: Which statements about Mockito's verify() are correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is used to verify interactions with a mocked object (e.g., how many times a method was called).

options.label: B
options.text: Mockito.times(1) is the default verification count.

options.label: C
options.text: verifyNoMoreInteractions ensures no additional calls occurred on the mocks.

options.label: D
options.text: verify() can only be used with @SpringBootTest.

explanation: Slide 22 shows all of these; verify() does not require @SpringBootTest.

question: What is the typical use of @SpyBean in Spring Boot?

options.label: A
options.text: Replacing a real bean with a spy to verify behavior, typically in integration testing.

options.label: B
options.text: Creating a fresh mock with no underlying real object.

options.label: C
options.text: Replacing JUnit with Mockito as the runner.

explanation: Slide 25 states @SpyBean is used to replace a real bean with a spy, typically for integration tests.

question: Which libraries are imported by the spring-boot-starter-test starter?
explanation: Slide 27 lists JUnit 5, Spring Test, AssertJ, Hamcrest, Mockito, JSONassert, JsonPath, and Awaitility.

question: What does the @DirtiesContext annotation do?

options.label: A
options.text: It causes the Spring application context cache to be invalidated, reducing performance optimization.

options.label: B
options.text: It marks a test method as failed automatically.

options.label: C
options.text: It permanently disables caching globally for the JVM.

options.label: D
options.text: It changes the JUnit runner used by the test.

explanation: Slide 28 explains @DirtiesContext manages the context cache but reduces performance optimization.

question: How do you run @SpringBootTest with an actual embedded server on a random port?
explanation: Slide 31 shows @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT).

question: Which beans are INCLUDED in a @WebMvcTest slice?
explanation: Slide 35: @WebMvcTest includes Controller-related beans but NOT @Service, @Component, @Repository.

question: Which beans are INCLUDED in @DataJpaTest?
explanation: Slide 35 lists @Repository, EntityManager, TestEntityManager, and DataSource as included in @DataJpaTest.

question: What is the default transactional behavior of @DataJpaTest?

options.label: A
options.text: Tests are transactional and roll back at the end of each test.

options.label: C
options.text: Tests do not run in any transaction.

options.label: D
options.text: Tests automatically truncate all tables before each method.

explanation: Slide 38: by default data JPA tests are transactional and roll back at the end of each test.

question: Which slice annotation is used for testing HTTP client code with a local server?
explanation: Slide 36: @RestClientTest is for testing HTTP client with a local server (includes RestTemplateBuilder, MockRestServiceServer).

question: Which statements about Spring profiles in testing are correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Profiles allow mapping beans to different scenarios (e.g., testing or development).

options.label: B
options.text: For Spring Boot tests, a profile can be activated using @ActiveProfiles("profilename").

options.label: C
options.text: Active profiles can be specified via JVM system parameter -Dspring.profiles.active=dev.

options.label: D
options.text: Profiles can only be configured via web.xml.

explanation: Slide 40 lists these activation methods; web.xml is only one of several.

question: Which three types of transactions are listed in Spring Boot test transactional support?
explanation: Slide 43 lists exactly these three types.

question: Which TestTransaction operations are mentioned in the lecture?
explanation: Slide 45 lists isActive(), isFlaggedForRollback(), flagForCommit(), flagForRollback() but no restartGlobal().

question: Which Testcontainers annotations are shown in the Spring Boot example for Neo4j?
explanation: Slide 46 shows @Testcontainers, @Container, and @ServiceConnection; @AutoConfigureContainerRegistry was not mentioned.

question: Which deprecations/changes are highlighted for Spring Boot 4 testing?

options.label: C
options.text: New annotations live in different packages than the old ones

options.label: D
options.text: Mockito itself is removed from spring-boot-starter-test

explanation: Slide 53 lists exactly these deprecations; Mockito remains part of the starter.
